title: Sorting
description: Master Java sorting with Arrays.sort, Collections.sort, Comparator.comparing, and lambda comparators for interview problems.

sections.title: Arrays.sort for Primitives and Objects
sections.explanation: Arrays.sort(arr) sorts primitive arrays in ascending order using dual-pivot quicksort (O(n log n) average). For object arrays (String[], Integer[]), it uses TimSort (stable, O(n log n)). Sort a subrange with Arrays.sort(arr, fromIndex, toIndex). Import `java.util.Arrays`. For descending order on objects, use `Arrays.sort(arr, Collections.reverseOrder())`.

exercise.prompt: Sort int[] arr = {40, 10, 50, 20, 30} in ascending order and print it. Then create an Integer[] version of {40, 10, 50, 20, 30}, sort it in descending order using Collections.reverseOrder(), and print it.

validation.message: Use Arrays.sort() for ascending and Collections.reverseOrder() for descending.

exercise.hint: Arrays.sort(arr) sorts primitives ascending. Arrays.sort(boxed, Collections.reverseOrder()) sorts objects descending.

sections.title: Collections.sort for Lists
sections.explanation: Collections.sort(list) sorts an ArrayList in-place. It uses TimSort (stable). For natural order, just pass the list. For custom order, pass a Comparator as the second argument. Alternatively, use `list.sort(comparator)`. Both modify the list in place and return void. Works with any List implementation.

exercise.prompt: Create an ArrayList<String> with {"delta", "alpha", "charlie", "bravo"}. Sort alphabetically and print. Then sort by string length (shortest first) and print.

validation.expected:
[alpha, bravo, charlie, delta]
[alpha, bravo, delta, charlie]

validation.message: Use Collections.sort() for both, with a length-based comparator for the second sort.

exercise.hint: For length sort: Collections.sort(words, (a, b) -> a.length() - b.length()) or use Comparator.comparingInt(String::length).

sections.title: Comparator.comparing and Chaining
sections.explanation: Comparator.comparing(keyExtractor) creates a Comparator from a function. Chain with `.thenComparing()` for multi-key sorts. Use `.reversed()` for descending. `Comparator.comparingInt()` avoids boxing for int keys. These are cleaner than writing (a, b) -> ... lambdas for complex sorts. Import `java.util.Comparator`.

exercise.prompt: Create a list of strings: {"pear", "fig", "apple", "kiwi", "banana"}. Sort by length ascending, breaking ties alphabetically. Print the sorted list. Then sort by length descending and print.

validation.expected:
[fig, kiwi, pear, apple, banana]
[banana, apple, kiwi, pear, fig]
validation.message: Length asc with alpha ties: [fig,kiwi,pear,apple,banana]. Length desc: [banana,apple,kiwi,pear,fig].
Use Comparator.comparingInt with thenComparing and reversed().

exercise.hint: Comparator.comparingInt(String::length).thenComparing(Comparator.naturalOrder()) for ascending with ties. Add .reversed() after the chain is incorrect—reverse the length part only or rebuild for descending.

sections.title: Lambda Comparators for Custom Sorting
sections.explanation: For quick custom sorts, use lambda comparators: `(a, b) -> expression`. Return negative if a < b, zero if equal, positive if a > b. Common patterns: `(a, b) -> a - b` (ascending int), `(a, b) -> b - a` (descending int), `(a, b) -> a.length() - b.length()` (by string length). Warning: `a - b` can overflow for large ints; use `Integer.compare(a, b)` for safety.

exercise.prompt: Given a 2D array of [name_length, score] pairs represented as int[][] data = {{3,90},{5,85},{3,95},{5,80},{4,90}}, sort by first element ascending, then by second element descending for ties. Print each pair on its own line using Arrays.toString.

validation.message: Sorted by col 0 ascending, col 1 descending for ties.
Use Arrays.sort with a lambda comparator.

exercise.hint: Lambda: (a, b) -> a[0] != b[0] ? a[0] - b[0] : b[1] - a[1]. First element ascending, second descending.
